title: \included entity
text: \included entity An entity that is defined as a field in another entity, but which has no keys or relationships. Note: Avoid using included entities. This functionality is available to support existing databases in which included entities have already been defined. Included entities have the following characteristics: They must be in the variable-length part of the record. Therefore, fields in an included entity cannot be indexed. They are a physical part of the parent entity, so it is not possible to retrieve individual occurrences of the included entity separately from the parent entity. There is no separate I/O performed for included entities, and included entities cannot have any key definitions. All entity-level I/O ProcScript modules for included entities are ignored. An included entity may itself contain an included entity, but you cannot define subtypes for an included entity. Related Links entity A development object that defines a logical data aggregation and its associated default characteristics and behavior. It may be mapped to a table in a database, but it can also represent transient data, a collection of controls, or constant data. relationship An association between two entities, which is defined using primary or candidate key fields in both entities. down entity In a component structure, an inner entity that represents the many side in a one-to-many relationship with the outer entity. So called because one usually travels down a model diagram to go from the one entity to the many entity. By default, Uniface supports full create, read, update, and delete operations on both entities. up entity In a component structure, an inner entity that represents the one side in a one-to-many relationship with the outer entity. Deleting the outer entity is subject to delete constraints. Was this topic helpful? Like Dislike
